Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

21. Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  • Mechanism (cytochrome oxidase, histotoxic hypoxia)
  • Clinical features
  • Labs (hallmark triad)
  • Antidotes: hydroxocobalamin (preferred) vs nitrite/thiosulfate (contraindicated in CO)
  • Fire victim algorithm
  • Nitroprusside toxicity
  • Supportive care

Short Note Skeleton

  • Hallmark labs box
  • Hydroxocobalamin vs nitrite comparison
  • Fire victim algorithm
  • Nitroprusside box

Viva One-Liners

  • "CN: cytochrome oxidase inhibition → histotoxic hypoxia"
  • "Labs: high anion gap + HIGH VENOUS O₂ + lactate > 10"
  • "Hydroxocobalamin 5g IV: preferred, NO metHb, SAFE in CO"
  • "Nitrites: CONTRAINDICATED in fire/CO (MetHb + COHb = fatal)"
  • "Fire victim: 100% O₂ + hydroxocobalamin (assume CO + CN)"
  • "Lactate > 10 in smoke inhalation = CN until proven otherwise"
  • "Venous blood bright red = CN (or CO)"
  • "Bitter almond smell: only 40% detect"
  • "Nitroprusside toxicity: thiocyanate → hydroxocobalamin + dialysis"
  • "Hydroxocobalamin red urine: harmless, interferes with colorimetric labs"

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • Smoke inhalation patient: lactate 14, COHb 20% → hydroxocobalamin + 100% O₂ + HBO for CO
  • Nitroprusside infusion 48h, renal failure, acidosis → stop nitroprusside, hydroxocobalamin, dialysis
  • Industrial worker sudden collapse, bitter almond smell → hydroxocobalamin immediately

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

  • CN: Cytochrome oxidase inhibition
  • Labs: Anion gap + Venous O2 high + Lactate >10
  • Hydroxocobalamin: 5g IV, No MetHb, Safe in CO
  • Nitrites: Contraindicated in Fire/CO
  • Fire: CO + CN → 100% O2 + Hydroxocobalamin
  • Lactate >10 = CN
  • Nitroprusside: Thiocyanate
  • Red urine = Hydroxocobalamin

22. Summary

Cyanide poisoning = cytochrome c oxidase inhibition → histotoxic hypoxia. Hallmark labs: high anion gap metabolic acidosis + high venous O₂ + lactate > 10. Hydroxocobalamin 5g IV = preferred antidote (no metHb, safe in CO). Nitrites CONTRAINDICATED in fire/CO (metHb + COHb = fatal hypoxia). Fire victims: assume CO + CN → 100% O₂ + hydroxocobalamin. Bitter almond smell ≤ 40% detect. Nitroprusside toxicity → thiocyanate → hydroxocobalamin + dialysis.

23. MCQs (10)

  1. Cyanide mechanism of toxicity?

A. Methemoglobin formation

B. Inhibits cytochrome c oxidase (Complex IV) → cellular hypoxia

C. Hemoglobin binding

D. Pulmonary edema

Answer: B

Explanation: CN⁻ binds ferric iron (Fe³⁺) in cytochrome c oxidase (Complex IV) → blocks electron transport → cellular hypoxia (histotoxic hypoxia). High O₂ extraction failure → venous blood bright red.

  1. Cyanide classic presentation?

A. Cherry red skin, metabolic acidosis, coma

B. Cyanosis, respiratory alkalosis

C. Mydriasis, tachycardia

D. Only seizures

Answer: A

Explanation: CN⁻: 'cherry red' skin (rare, post-mortem), severe metabolic acidosis (lactic), coma, seizures, cardiovascular collapse. Venous blood bright red (high O₂ content, not extracted). Bitter almond odor (genetic).

  1. Hydroxocobalamin dose for cyanide?

A. 1 g IV

B. 5 g IV (2.5g/100mL over 15 min)

C. 10 g IV

D. 2.5 g IV

Answer: B

Explanation: Hydroxocobalamin 5g IV (2.5g/100mL over 15 min). Binds CN⁻ → cyanocobalamin (excreted renally). Preferred antidote (no metHb). Skin/r urine turns red. Interferes with lab assays (color).

  1. Nitrite + thiosulfate alternative - mechanism?

A. Nitrite forms metHb → binds CN⁻; thiosulfate → rhodanese → thiocyanate (renally excreted)

B. Both bind CN⁻ directly

C. Nitrite displaces CN⁻ from cytochrome

D. Thiosulfate reactivates cytochrome

Answer: A

Explanation: Nitrites (amyl/sodium): oxidize Hb → metHb → binds CN⁻ (forming cyanmetHb). Thiosulfate: sulfur donor for rhodanese → converts CN⁻ → thiocyanate (renal excretion). Risk: metHbemia, hypotension.

  1. Cyanide + carbon monoxide co-exposure?

A. Treat CN⁻ only

B. House fires: both common. Hydroxocobalamin for CN⁻ + 100% O₂ for CO. Avoid nitrites (worsen O₂ delivery)

C. Nitrites help both

D. HBO contraindicated

Answer: B

Explanation: House fires: CO + CN⁻ (plastics, wool, silk). Hydroxocobalamin preferred for CN⁻ (no metHb). 100% O₂ for CO. Nitrites worsen O₂ delivery (metHb + COHb). HBO for CO if indicated.

  1. Cyanide - lactic acidosis mechanism?

A. Anaerobic metabolism from cellular hypoxia (cytochrome oxidase inhibition)

B. Seizures only

C. Liver failure

D. Renal failure

Answer: A

Explanation: CN⁻ blocks cytochrome c oxidase → cells can't use O₂ → anaerobic metabolism → lactic acidosis. Venous O₂ high (bright red blood). Lactate > 10 = strong CN⁻ indicator.

  1. Hydroxocobalamin adverse effects?

A. Methemoglobinemia

B. Red skin/urine, hypertension, interference with lab assays (colorimetric)

C. Seizures

D. Hypotension

Answer: B

Explanation: Hydroxocobalamin: skin/urine turns red (transient), transient hypertension, interferes with colorimetric lab assays (creatinine, bilirubin, glucose, etc.). No metHb.

  1. Cyanide - amyl nitrite pearls?

A. Oral

B. Inhalation (break pearl, inhale vapor q1-2min)

C. IV

D. IM

Answer: B

Explanation: Amyl nitrite: break pearl, inhale vapor for 30 sec q1-2min. Pre-hospital/bridging to IV nitrites. MetHb formation → binds CN⁻.

  1. Cyanide - thiosulfate dose?

A. 1 g IV

B. 12.5 g (50 mL of 25%) IV over 10 min

C. 100 mg IV

D. 50 mg/kg

Answer: B

Explanation: Sodium thiosulfate: 12.5 g (50 mL of 25%) IV over 10 min. Can repeat half dose. Sulfur donor for rhodanese. Give AFTER nitrites (if using nitrite/thiosulfate regimen).

  1. Cyanide - hydroxycobalamin vs nitrites in smoke inhalation?

A. Nitrites preferred

B. Hydroxocobalamin preferred (no metHb, COHb already impairing O₂ delivery)

C. Equal

D. Nitrites only

Answer: B

Explanation: Smoke inhalation = CO + CN⁻. Hydroxocobalamin preferred: no metHb (nitrites worsen O₂ delivery with COHb). 100% O₂ for CO. HBO if CO criteria met.

24. SBA Questions (10)

  1. House fire victim. Unconscious, soot in airway, lactate 14, venous blood bright red. COHb 30%. Management?

A. Hydroxocobalamin 5g IV + 100% O₂ NRB + arrange HBO

B. Nitrites + thiosulfate + 100% O₂

C. 100% O₂ only

D. HBO only

Answer: A

Explanation: Smoke inhalation: CO + CN⁻. Lactate > 10 = CN⁻. Hydroxocobalamin 5g IV (no metHb). 100% O₂ for CO. HBO for CO (COHb >25%, LOC). Avoid nitrites (metHb + COHb = worse O₂ delivery).

  1. Industrial worker, sodium cyanide spill. Collapse, seizures, metabolic acidosis. Hydroxocobalamin not available. Alternative?

A. Amyl nitrite pearls (inhale) → sodium nitrite 300mg IV → sodium thiosulfate 12.5g IV

B. 100% O₂ only

C. Methylene blue

D. Naloxone

Answer: A

Explanation: Nitrite/thiosulfate regimen: amyl nitrite inhale (bridge) → sodium nitrite 300mg IV (10mg/kg child) over 5-10min → sodium thiosulfate 12.5g IV. Monitor metHb (keep <30%). Hydroxocobalamin preferred if available.

  1. Cyanide poisoning - hydroxycobalamin given. Lab shows creatinine 300 (baseline 80). Why?

A. Acute kidney injury from CN⁻

B. Assay interference (red color) - falsely elevated creatinine

C. Rhabdomyolysis

D. Dehydration

Answer: B

Explanation: Hydroxocobalamin turns plasma/urine red → interferes with COLORIMETRIC assays: creatinine, bilirubin, glucose, COHb, metHb, etc. Use non-colorimetric methods or interpret with caution.

  1. CN⁻ poisoning - venous blood gas shows pO₂ 80 mmHg (high). Why?

A. Hyperventilation

B. Cells can't extract O₂ (cytochrome oxidase blocked) → venous O₂ remains high

C. Sampling error

D. CO poisoning

Answer: B

Explanation: CN⁻ blocks cytochrome c oxidase → cells can't utilize O₂ → venous blood retains high O₂ content → bright red, high venous pO₂. Classic finding.

  1. Child finds cyanide salts, ingests. Rapid collapse. Antidote?

A. Hydroxocobalamin 5g IV (weight-based 70mg/kg max 5g)

B. Nitrites only

C. Thiosulfate only

D. Supportive only

Answer: A

Explanation: Hydroxocobalamin: pediatric dose 70 mg/kg IV (max 5g). Same for nitrite/thiosulfate (nitrite 10mg/kg, thiosulfate 400mg/kg). Rapid administration critical.

  1. CN⁻ - metHb level after nitrites should be kept?

A. < 10%

B. < 30%

C. < 50%

D. No limit

Answer: B

Explanation: Nitrites → metHb (binds CN⁻). Keep metHb < 30% (monitor). Higher = impaired O₂ delivery. Sodium nitrite 300mg IV → metHb ~10-20%.

  1. Hydroxocobalamin - blood pressure effect?

A. Hypotension

B. Transient hypertension

C. No effect

D. Bradycardia

Answer: B

Explanation: Hydroxocobalamin causes transient hypertension (mechanism unclear, possibly NO scavenging). Monitor BP. Resolves spontaneously.

  1. Cyanide - rhodanese enzyme?

A. Converts CN⁻ to cyanocobalamin

B. Converts CN⁻ + thiosulfate → thiocyanate (renal excretion)

C. Reactivates cytochrome oxidase

D. Binds CN⁻ in blood

Answer: B

Explanation: Rhodanese (sulfurtransferase): uses thiosulfate as sulfur donor → converts CN⁻ → thiocyanate (SCN⁻) → renal excretion. Thiosulfate provides sulfur.

  1. Cyanide - disposition after hydroxocobalamin?

A. Discharge if asymptomatic

B. Observe 24h (rebound toxicity possible, monitor lactate, neurologic)

C. 6h observation

D. ICU only if intubated

Answer: B

Explanation: Observe 24h: CN⁻ can redistribute, rebound toxicity. Monitor lactate, neurologic status, cardiac. Psych if DSH.

25. Flashcards

  • Q: Cyanide mechanism?

A: Binds Fe³⁺ in cytochrome c oxidase (Complex IV) → blocks electron transport → cellular (histotoxic) hypoxia. Cells can't use O₂.

  • Q: CN⁻ classic findings?

A: Metabolic acidosis (lactic), coma, seizures, CV collapse. Venous blood BRIGHT RED (high O₂). Cherry red skin (rare, post-mortem). Bitter almond odor (genetic).

  • Q: Lactate > 10 in smoke inhalation?

A: Strong indicator of CN⁻ poisoning (cellular hypoxia → anaerobic metabolism).

  • Q: Hydroxocobalamin?

A: 5g IV (2.5g/100mL over 15min). Binds CN⁻ → cyanocobalamin (renal excretion). Preferred: no metHb. Skin/urine red. Lab assay interference.

  • Q: Nitrite + thiosulfate?

A: Nitrite → metHb (binds CN⁻). Thiosulfate → rhodanese → thiocyanate (renal). Keep metHb <30%. Hypotension risk.

  • Q: CN⁻ + CO co-exposure?

A: House fires: both. Hydroxocobalamin for CN⁻ (no metHb) + 100% O₂ for CO. Avoid nitrites (worsen O₂ delivery). HBO for CO if indicated.

  • Q: CN⁻ venous blood gas?

A: High venous pO₂ (cells can't extract O₂). Bright red venous blood. Normal/high pO₂ with severe acidosis.

  • Q: Hydroxocobalamin lab interference?

A: Red color interferes with COLORIMETRIC assays: creatinine, bilirubin, glucose, COHb, metHb, etc. Use non-colorimetric methods.

  • Q: Amyl nitrite pearls?

A: Inhalation: break pearl, inhale vapor 30 sec q1-2min. Bridge to IV nitrites. MetHb formation.

  • Q: Thiosulfate dose?

A: 12.5g (50mL 25%) IV over 10min. Sulfur donor for rhodanese. Give after nitrites (if using that regimen).

  • Q: Pediatric CN⁻ antidote?

A: Hydroxocobalamin 70mg/kg IV (max 5g). Nitrite 10mg/kg. Thiosulfate 400mg/kg.

  • Q: MetHb target with nitrites?

A: < 30%. Monitor. Sodium nitrite 300mg IV → ~10-20% metHb.

  • Q: Hydroxocobalamin BP effect?

A: Transient hypertension. Monitor. Resolves spontaneously.

  • Q: Rhodanese?

A: Sulfurtransferase: CN⁻ + thiosulfate → thiocyanate (SCN⁻) → renal excretion. Thiosulfate = sulfur donor.

  • Q: CN⁻ disposition?

A: Observe 24h (rebound toxicity possible). Monitor lactate, neuro, cardiac. Psych if DSH.

26. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B - CN⁻ binds ferric iron (Fe³⁺) in cytochrome c oxidase (Complex IV) → blocks electron transport → cellular hypoxia (histotoxic hypoxia). High O₂ extraction failure → venous blood bright red.
  2. A - CN⁻: 'cherry red' skin (rare, post-mortem), severe metabolic acidosis (lactic), coma, seizures, cardiovascular collapse. Venous blood bright red (high O₂ content, not extracted). Bitter almond odor (genetic).
  3. B - Hydroxocobalamin 5g IV (2.5g/100mL over 15 min). Binds CN⁻ → cyanocobalamin (excreted renally). Preferred antidote (no metHb). Skin/r urine turns red. Interferes with lab assays (color).
  4. A - Nitrites (amyl/sodium): oxidize Hb → metHb → binds CN⁻ (forming cyanmetHb). Thiosulfate: sulfur donor for rhodanese → converts CN⁻ → thiocyanate (renal excretion). Risk: metHbemia, hypotension.
  5. B - House fires: CO + CN⁻ (plastics, wool, silk). Hydroxocobalamin preferred for CN⁻ (no metHb). 100% O₂ for CO. Nitrites worsen O₂ delivery (metHb + COHb). HBO for CO if indicated.
  6. A - CN⁻ blocks cytochrome c oxidase → cells can't use O₂ → anaerobic metabolism → lactic acidosis. Venous O₂ high (bright red blood). Lactate > 10 = strong CN⁻ indicator.
  7. B - Hydroxocobalamin: skin/urine turns red (transient), transient hypertension, interferes with colorimetric lab assays (creatinine, bilirubin, glucose, etc.). No metHb.
  8. B - Amyl nitrite: break pearl, inhale vapor for 30 sec q1-2min. Pre-hospital/bridging to IV nitrites. MetHb formation → binds CN⁻.
  9. B - Sodium thiosulfate: 12.5 g (50 mL of 25%) IV over 10 min. Can repeat half dose. Sulfur donor for rhodanese. Give AFTER nitrites (if using nitrite/thiosulfate regimen).
  10. B - Smoke inhalation = CO + CN⁻. Hydroxocobalamin preferred: no metHb (nitrites worsen O₂ delivery with COHb). 100% O₂ for CO. HBO if CO criteria met.

SBAs

  1. A - Smoke inhalation: CO + CN⁻. Lactate > 10 = CN⁻. Hydroxocobalamin 5g IV (no metHb). 100% O₂ for CO. HBO for CO (COHb >25%, LOC). Avoid nitrites (metHb + COHb = worse O₂ delivery).
  2. A - Nitrite/thiosulfate regimen: amyl nitrite inhale (bridge) → sodium nitrite 300mg IV (10mg/kg child) over 5-10min → sodium thiosulfate 12.5g IV. Monitor metHb (keep <30%). Hydroxocobalamin preferred if available.
  3. B - Hydroxocobalamin turns plasma/urine red → interferes with COLORIMETRIC assays: creatinine, bilirubin, glucose, COHb, metHb, etc. Use non-colorimetric methods or interpret with caution.
  4. B - CN⁻ blocks cytochrome c oxidase → cells can't utilize O₂ → venous blood retains high O₂ content → bright red, high venous pO₂. Classic finding.
  5. A - Hydroxocobalamin: pediatric dose 70 mg/kg IV (max 5g). Same for nitrite/thiosulfate (nitrite 10mg/kg, thiosulfate 400mg/kg). Rapid administration critical.
  6. B - Nitrites → metHb (binds CN⁻). Keep metHb < 30% (monitor). Higher = impaired O₂ delivery. Sodium nitrite 300mg IV → metHb ~10-20%.
  7. B - Hydroxocobalamin causes transient hypertension (mechanism unclear, possibly NO scavenging). Monitor BP. Resolves spontaneously.
  8. B - Rhodanese (sulfurtransferase): uses thiosulfate as sulfur donor → converts CN⁻ → thiocyanate (SCN⁻) → renal excretion. Thiosulfate provides sulfur.
  9. B - Observe 24h: CN⁻ can redistribute, rebound toxicity. Monitor lactate, neurologic status, cardiac. Psych if DSH.

topics.title: Digoxin Poisoning
topics.html:
Related: [[General Principles of Poisoning Management]], [[Antidotes Overview]], [[Beta-Blocker Poisoning]], [[Calcium Channel Blocker Poisoning]], [[Hyperkalemia]], [[High-Dose Insulin Euglycemia Therapy (HIET)]]

[!tip]
Digoxin-specific Fab fragments = definitive antidote. Hyperkalemia in acute digoxin poisoning = poor prognosis, treat with Fab FIRST (not insulin/dextrose/calcium). Key FCPS/MRCP: Fab dosing (empirical 10-20 vials or calculated: serum level × weight / 100); avoid calcium (stone heart); treat arrhythmias with Fab ± Mg/phenytoin/lidocaine; chronic toxicity at lower levels; renal impairment ↑ risk.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognize digoxin toxicity (GI, cardiac, visual, neurologic)
  • Calculate and administer digoxin-specific Fab fragments
  • Interpret hyperkalemia as marker of severity (acute)
  • Avoid contraindicated treatments (calcium, atropine for AV block)
  • Differentiate acute vs chronic toxicity
  • Manage arrhythmias in digoxin toxicity

2. Definition

Digoxin poisoning = toxicity from cardiac glycoside (digoxin, digitoxin) causing Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase inhibition → ↑ intracellular Na⁺ → ↓ Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchange → ↑ intracellular Ca²⁺ → increased automaticity + decreased conduction → arrhythmias.

3. Core Physiology

  • Mechanism: binds Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase (α subunit) → inhibition → ↑ intracellular Na⁺ → ↓ Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger (NCX) activity → ↑ intracellular Ca²⁺
  • Effects:
  • Positive inotropy (therapeutic) → at toxic doses: delayed afterdepolarizations (DADs) → triggered activity → tachyarrhythmias
  • Increased vagal tone → bradycardia, AV block (therapeutic and toxic)
  • Pharmacokinetics: narrow therapeutic index (0.5-2 ng/mL), large Vd (~500 L), renal excretion (60-80% unchanged), long half-life 36-48h (prolonged in renal failure), tissue binding → levels don't correlate acutely with severity (distribution phase)
  • Risk factors: renal failure, elderly, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypercalcemia, hypothyroidism, amiodarone/verapamil/diltiazem/quinidine (↑ digoxin level), acute illness

4. Clinical Features

Gastrointestinal (Most Common, Early)

  • Nausea, vomiting, anorexia, abdominal pain

Cardiac (Most Serious)

  • Arrhythmias — "virtually any arrhythmia except rapidly conducted atrial arrhythmias":
  • Bradyarrhythmias: sinus brady, SA block, AV block (1°, 2°, 3°), junctional rhythms
  • Tachyarrhythmias: PAT with block (classic), atrial tachycardia, bidirectional ventricular tachycardia (pathognomonic), VT, VF
  • Ectopy: frequent PVCs, bigeminy, trigeminy
  • Hyperkalemia (acute poisoning) — marker of severity, poor prognosis

Visual (Classic)

  • Yellow-green vision (xanthopsia), blurred vision, photophobia, scotomas

Neurologic

  • Confusion, delirium, headache, fatigue, weakness

Acute vs Chronic Toxicity

FeatureAcute (Intentional OD)Chronic (Therapeutic Misadventure)
Serum levelVery high (> 10 ng/mL)Mildly elevated (2.5-4 ng/mL)
PotassiumMarked hyperkalemia (acute release)Normal/mild ↑
Renal functionInitially normalOften impaired (precipitant)
Fab doseHigher (empirical 15-20 vials)Lower (calculated or 5-10 vials)
PrognosisBetter with FabWorse (comorbidities, delayed Dx)

5. Investigations

  • Digoxin level — drawn ≥ 6h post-dose (distribution phase); does not correlate acutely with severity in acute OD; therapeutic 0.5-2 ng/mL; toxic > 2 ng/mL
  • Potassium — CRITICAL in acute OD (hyperkalemia = severe)
  • Renal function — creatinine, eGFR
  • Electrolytes — Mg²⁺ (hypomagnesemia ↑ toxicity), Ca²⁺
  • ECG — arrhythmias, PR prolongation, ST sag ("reverse tick" / "Salvador Dali mustache")
  • Mg²⁺ — hypomagnesemia common, worsens toxicity
  • Paracetamol level (always)

6. Management

1. Resuscitation (ABCDE)

  • Airway/Breathing: standard
  • Circulation: treat arrhythmias, hypotension

2. Digoxin-Specific Antibody Fragments (Digoxin Immune Fab / DigiFab / Digibind) — DEFINITIVE ANTIDOTE

  • Mechanism: binds free digoxin in plasma → ↑ Vd → pulls digoxin from tissue → complex renally excreted (dialysis if anuric)
  • Indications (Any One):
  • Life-threatening arrhythmia (VF, sustained VT, high-grade AV block, symptomatic bradycardia)
  • Hyperkalemia > 5.5 mmol/L (acute)
  • Serum level > 10 ng/mL (acute) or > 6 ng/mL (chronic with toxicity)
  • Ingestion > 10 mg (adult) or > 4 mg (child) or unknown large amount
  • Cardiac arrest (if digoxin suspected)
  • Dosing:
  • Empirical (preferred in acute/severe): 10-20 vials (each vial binds ~0.5 mg digoxin)
  • Cardiac arrest / severe: 20 vials
  • Significant toxicity: 10-15 vials
  • Calculated (if level known, steady state):
  • Vials = (Serum digoxin ng/mL × Weight kg) / 100 (round up)
  • Example: 5 ng/mL × 70 kg / 100 = 3.5 → 4 vials
  • Administration: reconstitute each vial with 4 mL sterile water → IV infusion over 30 min (can push in cardiac arrest)
  • Onset: 30-60 min for arrhythmia resolution; K⁺ falls within 1-2h
  • Monitoring: K⁺ q1-2h (rebound hypokalemia as digoxin redistributes), digoxin level at 24h (assay measures total = free + bound; interpret with caution — free fraction low), renal function
  • Renal failure: Fab-digoxin complex accumulates → may need hemodialysis for complex removal; consider repeat Fab doses

3. Hyperkalemia in Acute Digoxin Poisoning — TREAT WITH FAB, NOT STANDARD MEASURES

  • Mechanism: Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase inhibition → K⁺ cannot enter cells → severe hyperkalemia (can be > 7-8 mmol/L)
  • Management: DIGOXIN FAB FIRST — rapidly lowers K⁺ as digoxin redistributes
  • Do NOT give: insulin/dextrose, calcium, beta-agonists, kayexalate as primary — delays Fab, calcium may cause "stone heart"
  • If Fab unavailable + life-threatening hyperkalemia: standard measures with extreme caution (no calcium)

4. Arrhythmia Management

  • Primary: Digoxin Fab — resolves most arrhythmias
  • Bradydysrhythmias (symptomatic brady, high-grade AV block):
  • Atropine 0.5 mg IV — may be ineffective (vagal + direct effect)
  • Transcutaneous pacing — often ineffective (increased automaticity competes)
  • Isoproterenol — consider if pacing fails (increases automaticity/conduction)
  • AVOID CALCIUM — "stone heart" (irreversible contracture from massive Ca²⁺ influx)
  • Tachydysrhythmias (PAT with block, bidirectional VT, VT/VF):
  • Magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 10-20 min — first-line adjunct (suppresses DADs)
  • Phenytoin 15 mg/kg IV — acceptable (Class Ib, shortens action potential, suppresses DADs) — unlike TCA/BB/CCB
  • Lidocaine 1-1.5 mg/kg IV — acceptable alternative
  • AVOID: amiodarone (↑ digoxin level, may worsen), verapamil/diltiazem, beta-blockers, procainamide, quinidine
  • Cardioversion — avoid if possible (↑ risk VF); if unstable, lowest energy (1-5 J) + Fab simultaneously

5. Hypokalemia / Hypomagnesemia

  • Correct K⁺ to > 4.0 mmol/L (hypokalemia ↑ digoxin binding to ATPase)
  • Correct Mg²⁺ to > 0.8 mmol/L

6. Decontamination

  • Activated charcoal: 1 g/kg if < 1-2h (not well adsorbed but may help)
  • Cholestyramine — interrupts enterohepatic circulation (chronic toxicity)

7. Monitoring & Disposition

  • Continuous ECG until arrhythmias resolved 24h
  • K⁺ q1-2h post-Fab (rebound hypokalemia)
  • Digoxin level at 24h (interpret with caution — assay measures bound+free)
  • Renal function
  • Observe 24h+ post-Fab
  • Psych assessment (if intentional)

7. Complications

  • Ventricular fibrillation / asystole
  • "Stone heart" (calcium-induced)
  • Rebound toxicity (Fab-digoxin complex dissociates in renal failure)
  • Hypokalemia post-Fab
  • Anaphylaxis to Fab (rare, sheep antibody)

8. Prognosis

  • Excellent with Fab — mortality < 1-2% if Fab given
  • Without Fab: mortality 5-10% (acute), higher chronic
  • Chronic toxicity: worse due to comorbidities, delayed recognition

9. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  1. Digoxin Fab = definitive antidote — indications: life-threatening arrhythmia, K⁺ > 5.5, level > 10 (acute) / > 6 (chronic), ingestion > 10 mg
  2. Fab dosing: empirical 10-20 vials (acute/severe) OR calculated (level × weight / 100)
  3. Hyperkalemia in acute OD = treat with Fab FIRST — NOT insulin/dextrose/calcium
  4. Hyperkalemia = poor prognostic marker in acute poisoning
  5. AVOID CALCIUM — "stone heart" (irreversible contracture)
  6. Atropine/pacing often ineffective for bradyarrhythmias
  7. Bidirectional VT = pathognomonic for digoxin toxicity
  8. PAT with block = classic (atrial tachycardia with AV block)
  9. Chronic toxicity at lower levels (2.5-4 ng/mL) — elderly, renal failure, drug interactions
  10. Renal failure → prolonged half-life, Fab complex accumulation → may need dialysis
  11. Correct hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia (↑ digoxin binding)
  12. Phenytoin/lidocaine acceptable for tachyarrhythmias (unlike TCA/BB/CCB)
  13. Avoid amiodarone (↑ digoxin level, may worsen)

10. Common Viva Questions

  1. Digoxin Fab indications and dosing (empirical vs calculated)
  2. Hyperkalemia management in acute digoxin poisoning
  3. Why avoid calcium? ("Stone heart")
  4. Arrhythmia management (brady vs tachy)
  5. Acute vs chronic toxicity differences
  6. Bidirectional VT / PAT with block
  7. Drug interactions increasing digoxin level
  8. Monitoring post-Fab (K⁺ rebound, level interpretation)

11. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • Treat hyperkalemia with insulin/dextrose/calcium → NO, FAB FIRST
  • Calcium for hyperkalemia in digoxin → NO, "stone heart"
  • Atropine works for digoxin bradycardia → often ineffective
  • Pacing works → often ineffective
  • Amiodarone for digoxin VT → NO, ↑ digoxin level, worsens
  • Digoxin level correlates with severity acutely → NO, distribution phase
  • Chronic toxicity needs higher levels → NO, lower levels (2.5-4) with renal failure/drug interactions
  • Fab dose = level × weight → yes but empirical 10-20 vials often used in acute

12. Mnemonics

  • DIGOXIN FAB: Fab = Antidote for Binding digoxin
  • HYPERKALEMIA: FAB FIRST (not insulin/Ca)
  • STONE HEART: Calcium in Digoxin = Stone Heart
  • BIDIRECTIONAL VT: Bi-Directional = Digoxin Pathognomonic
  • PAT WITH BLOCK: Paroxysmal Atrial Tachycardia With Block = Digoxin Classic
  • FAB DOSE: Empirical 10-20 vials; Calculated = Level × Weight / 100
  • AVOID: Calcium, Amiodarone, Atropine (poor), Pacing (poor)

13. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Digoxin Poisoning))

Mechanism

Na/K-ATPase Inhibition

Intracellular Ca++ ↑

Delayed Afterdepolarizations

Increased Vagal Tone

Clinical

GI: Nausea, Vomiting (Early)

Cardiac: Any Arrhythmia (BRADY + TACHY)

Visual: Xanthopsia (Yellow-Green)

Neuro: Confusion

Acute vs Chronic

Acute: High Level, HyperK, Fab 10-20 vials

Chronic: Low Level, Normal K, Fab 5-10 vials

Antidote

Digoxin-Specific Fab

Indications: Arrhythmia, K>5.5, Level>10/6, Ingestion>10mg

Dose: Empirical 10-20 vials OR Level×Wt/100

HyperK

Acute: FAB FIRST (NO Ca, NO Insulin)

Chronic: Less Prominent

Arrhythmias

Brady: Fab, Atropine (Poor), Pacing (Poor), NO Ca

Tachy: Fab, MgSO4, Phenytoin/Lidocaine, NO Amiodarone

Cardioversion: Low Energy if Unstable

```

14. Flowchart

```mermaid

flowchart TD

A[Digoxin Toxicity Suspected] --> B[ABCDE + ECG + Digoxin Level\n+ K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Renal Function]

B --> C{Indications for Fab?}

C -->|Life-Threatening Arrhythmia\nK+ > 5.5\nLevel > 10 (Acute) / > 6 (Chronic)\nIngestion > 10mg| D[Digoxin Fab: Empirical 10-20 Vials\nOR Calculated: Level×Wt/100\nIV over 30 min]

C -->|No Indications| E[Monitor\nCorrect K+/Mg2+\nHold Digoxin]

D --> F[Monitor K+ q1-2h\nRebound Hypokalemia Expected\nECG Continuous]

F --> G{Arrhythmias?}

G -->|Bradydysrhythmia| H[Fab Primary\nAtropine (Often Fails)\nPacing (Often Fails)\nNO CALCIUM - Stone Heart]

G -->|Tachydysrhythmia| I[Fab Primary\nMgSO4 2g IV\nPhenytoin/Lidocaine\nNO Amiodarone\nCardioversion: Low Energy if Unstable]

G -->|Resolved| J[Observe 24h Post-Fab\nPsych Assessment]

H --> J

I --> J

```

15. Suggested Visuals / Image Notes

  • Bidirectional VT ECG
  • PAT with block ECG
  • Fab dosing calculator
  • Stone heart mechanism

16. Suggested Video References

  • Digoxin Fab administration
  • Digoxin toxicity arrhythmia recognition

17. One-Page Revision Summary

  • Mechanism: Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase inhibition → ↑ intracellular Ca²⁺ → DADs → arrhythmias + vagal ↑ → brady
  • Clinical: GI (early), cardiac (any arrhythmia), visual (xanthopsia), neuro
  • Acute: high level, hyperkalemia, Fab 10-20 vials
  • Chronic: lower level, normal K, Fab 5-10 vials
  • Fab: empirical 10-20 vials OR level×weight/100
  • HyperK acute: FAB FIRST — NO calcium, NO insulin/dextrose
  • Stone heart: calcium contraindicated
  • Arrhythmias: Fab primary; brady = atropine/pacing poor, NO Ca; tachy = Mg, phenytoin/lidocaine; NO amiodarone
  • Bidirectional VT / PAT with block = pathognomonic/classic
  • Correct K⁺ > 4.0, Mg²⁺ > 0.8
  • Renal failure: prolonged half-life, Fab complex accumulation → dialysis

24-Hour Recall Prompts

  • State Fab indications and dosing (empirical vs calculated)
  • Explain hyperkalemia management in acute digoxin poisoning
  • Why is calcium contraindicated? ("Stone heart")
  • List acceptable vs contraindicated antiarrhythmics

7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

  • [ ] Day 1 completed
  • [ ] 24-hour recall completed
  • [ ] Day 7 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 15 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 30 revision completed

18. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Fab indications (arrhythmia, K>5.5, level>10/6, ingestion>10mg)
  • Fab dosing: empirical 10-20 vials or calculated (level×wt/100)
  • Hyperkalemia acute = FAB FIRST (NO calcium, NO insulin/dextrose)
  • Calcium = stone heart (CONTRAINDICATED)
  • Bidirectional VT / PAT with block = digoxin
  • Chronic toxicity at lower levels (renal failure, drug interactions)
  • Phenytoin/lidocaine OK for tachy; atropine/pacing poor for brady
  • Avoid amiodarone

Should Know

  • Fab monitoring: K⁺ q1-2h (rebound hypokalemia), level at 24h (bound+free)
  • Renal failure: prolonged half-life, Fab complex dialysis
  • Drug interactions: amiodarone, verapamil, diltiazem, quinidine, macrolides
  • Cholestyramine for chronic

Nice to Know

  • Digitoxin (longer half-life, not dialyzable)
  • Oleander/yellow oleander (cardiac glycoside plants) — Fab works
  • Specific Fab products (DigiFab vs Digibind)
  • Cardioversion energy if unstable (1-5 J)

19. Self-Test Scorecard

  • Understanding: /10
  • Recall: /10
  • MCQ Performance: /10
  • SBA Performance: /10
  • Viva Confidence: /10
  • Total: /50
[!tip]
Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

20. Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  • Mechanism: Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase → ↑ intracellular Ca²⁺ → DADs + vagal
  • Clinical: GI, cardiac (brady + tachy), visual, neuro
  • Acute vs chronic comparison
  • Investigations (level timing, K⁺ critical)
  • Management: Fab (indications, dosing, monitoring), hyperK management, arrhythmia-specific
  • Contraindicated: calcium, amiodarone
  • Complications/prognosis

Short Note Skeleton

  • Fab indications box
  • HyperK algorithm (FAB FIRST)
  • Arrhythmia management table
  • Acute vs chronic table

Viva One-Liners

  • "Digoxin Fab: life-threatening arrhythmia, K>5.5, level>10/6, ingestion>10mg"
  • "Fab dose: empirical 10-20 vials OR level×weight/100"
  • "Hyperkalemia acute digoxin: FAB FIRST — NO calcium, NO insulin/dextrose"
  • "Calcium in digoxin = STONE HEART (contraindicated)"
  • "Bidirectional VT = pathognomonic digoxin"
  • "PAT with block = classic digoxin"
  • "Brady: Fab, atropine/pacing poor, NO Ca"
  • "Tachy: Fab, Mg, phenytoin/lidocaine, NO amiodarone"
  • "Chronic: lower levels (2.5-4), renal failure, drug interactions"
  • "Correct K>4.0, Mg>0.8"

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • Elderly on digoxin + amiodarone + renal failure → "therapeutic" level 2.8 = TOXIC
  • Acute OD with K⁺ 7.2 → FAB immediate, no calcium
  • Post-Fab monitoring: K⁺ drops → replace to > 4.0

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

  • Fab indications: Arrhythmia, K>5.5, Level>10/6, Ingest>10mg
  • Fab dose: 10-20 vials empirical / Level×Wt/100 calculated
  • HyperK: FAB FIRST (No Ca, No Insulin)
  • Ca = Stone Heart
  • Bidirectional VT / PAT with Block
  • Brady: Fab, No Ca
  • Tachy: Fab, Mg, Phenytoin/Lido
  • Chronic: Low level, renal, interactions
  • K>4.0, Mg>0.8

21. Summary

Digoxin poisoning = Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase inhibition → ↑ intracellular Ca²⁺ → arrhythmias (brady + tachy) + vagal effects. Digoxin-specific Fab = definitive antidote (empirical 10-20 vials or calculated). Hyperkalemia in acute = FAB FIRST (NO calcium — stone heart). Bidirectional VT / PAT with block pathognomonic. Chronic toxicity at lower levels (renal failure, drug interactions). Arrhythmias: brady = Fab, atropine/pacing poor, NO Ca; tachy = Fab, Mg, phenytoin/lidocaine, NO amiodarone. Correct K⁺ > 4.0, Mg²⁺ > 0.8.

22. MCQs (10)

  1. Digoxin mechanism of toxicity?

A. Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase inhibition → ↑ intracellular Na⁺ → ↑ Ca²⁺ via Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger

B. Beta-blockade

C. Calcium channel blockade

D. Potassium channel blockade

Answer: A

Explanation: Digoxin inhibits Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase → ↑ intracellular Na⁺ → ↑ Ca²⁺ via Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger (inotropy) but also ↑ automaticity, ↓ AV conduction, arrhythmias. Toxicity = same mechanism exaggerated.

  1. Digoxin toxicity - classic arrhythmias?

A. Only ventricular tachycardia

B. EVERY arrhythmia EXCEPT rapidly conducted atrial arrhythmias (AF with rapid ventricular response)

C. Only bradycardia

D. Only AV block

Answer: B

Explanation: Digoxin toxicity = 'every arrhythmia except rapidly conducted atrial arrhythmias'. Common: PVCs, bigeminy, VT/VF, AV block (any degree), atrial tachycardia with block, bidirectional VT (pathognomonic).

  1. Digoxin-specific Fab dose calculation?

A. Level × weight / 100 = vials

B. Serum level (ng/mL) × weight (kg) / 100 = vials OR empirical 10-20 vials

C. 1 vial per ng/mL

D. Fixed 5 vials

Answer: B

Explanation: Fab dose = (serum digoxin ng/mL × weight kg) / 100 = number of vials. If level unknown/acute massive: empirical 10-20 vials. Each vial binds ~0.5mg digoxin.

  1. Digoxin hyperkalemia - management?

A. Insulin/dextrose + calcium

B. Digoxin-specific Fab FIRST (reverses Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase block → K⁺ shifts into cells)

C. Sodium bicarbonate

D. Kayexalate

Answer: B

Explanation: Digoxin hyperkalemia = poor prognosis (massive Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase blockade → K⁺ can't enter cells). Give Fab FIRST. Standard hyperK measures can be dangerous (insulin → hypokalemia post-Fab).

  1. Bidirectional ventricular tachycardia - pathognomonic for?

A. TCA poisoning

B. Digoxin toxicity

C. Catecholaminergic polymorphic VT

D. Long QT

Answer: B

Explanation: Bidirectional VT (alternating QRS axis) = pathognomonic for digoxin toxicity. Also: atrial tachycardia with block (PAT with block).

  1. Digoxin toxicity precipitated by?

A. Hyperkalemia

B. Hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, renal failure, amiodarone, verapamil, quinidine, elderly

C. Hypercalcemia only

D. Hyponatremia

Answer: B

Explanation: Precipitants: hypokalemia (↑ digoxin binding), hypomagnesemia, renal failure (↓ clearance), drug interactions (amiodarone, verapamil, quinidine, dronedarone ↑ levels), elderly (↓ Vd, ↓ renal function).

  1. Digoxin Fab - when to give?

A. Any level > 2 ng/mL

B. Life-threatening arrhythmia, hemodynamic instability, K⁺ > 5.5, level > 10 ng/mL (acute) or > 6 ng/mL (chronic), ingest > 10mg (adult) or > 4mg (child)

C. Only if level > 15

D. Only for bradycardia

Answer: B

Explanation: Fab indications: life-threatening arrhythmia, hemodynamic instability, K⁺ > 5.5, level > 10 ng/mL (acute) or > 6 ng/mL (chronic), massive ingestion (>10mg adult, >4mg child).

  1. Digoxin toxicity - atropine for bradycardia?

A. First line

B. Often ineffective (not purely vagal), Fab is definitive

C. Contraindicated

D. Only for AV block

Answer: B

Explanation: Digoxin bradycardia/AV block = ↓ conduction (not vagal). Atropine often INEFFECTIVE. Fab is definitive. Temporary pacing if hemodynamically unstable awaiting Fab.

  1. Digoxin + hypokalemia - why dangerous?

A. ↑ Digoxin binding to Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase (K⁺ competes) → ↑ toxicity

B. Causes arrhythmias directly

C. Worsens renal function

D. No interaction

Answer: A

Explanation: K⁺ competes with digoxin for Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase binding. Hypokalemia → ↑ digoxin binding → ↑ toxicity. Correct K⁺ > 4.0 (but avoid overcorrection post-Fab).

  1. Digoxin level interpretation - acute vs chronic?

A. Same threshold

B. Acute: > 10 ng/mL toxic. Chronic: > 6 ng/mL toxic (tissue levels higher)

C. Chronic higher threshold

D. Levels not useful

Answer: B

Explanation: Acute: level > 10 ng/mL toxic. Chronic: > 6 ng/mL toxic (tissue levels higher than serum). Levels at 6h post-dose (distribution complete). Clinical context > level.

23. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 80yo on digoxin, furosemide. Presents with nausea, confusion, K⁺ 5.8, HR 40, ECG: bidirectional VT. Digoxin level 4.5 ng/mL. Management?

A. Insulin/dextrose for K⁺

B. Digoxin-specific Fab 10-20 vials empirically (life-threatening arrhythmia + hyperK)

C. Atropine + pacing

D. Lidocaine

Answer: B

Explanation: Life-threatening arrhythmia (bidirectional VT) + hyperK = Fab INDICATED. HyperK = Fab FIRST (reverses Na/K-ATPase block). Level 4.5 ng/mL in chronic toxicity = toxic (threshold >6 chronic, but K⁺ 5.8 = Fab indication). Empirical 10-20 vials.

  1. Toddler ingests 15 digoxin tablets (0.25mg each = 3.75mg). > 4mg child = massive ingestion. Management?

A. Observe

B. Fab 10-20 vials empirically (massive ingestion >4mg child)

C. Charcoal only

D. Atropine

Answer: B

Explanation: Child > 4mg ingestion = massive = Fab indicated empirically. Also: charcoal if <1h. Monitor for arrhythmias, hyperK. Level at 6h post-ingestion.

  1. Digoxin toxicity + hyperK 6.2. Give insulin/dextrose?

A. Yes, standard hyperK protocol

B. NO - Fab FIRST. Insulin can cause profound hypokalemia after Fab reverses Na/K blockade

C. Only if ECG changes

D. Calcium first

Answer: B

Explanation: Digoxin hyperK: Na/K-ATPase blocked → K⁺ can't enter cells. Fab reverses blockade → K⁺ shifts INTO cells rapidly. Insulin/dextrose can cause DANGEROUS hypokalemia post-Fab. Give Fab FIRST.

  1. Digoxin toxicity - atrial tachycardia with block (PAT with block). Significance?

A. Benign

B. Classic digoxin toxicity arrhythmia (↑ automaticity + ↓ AV conduction)

C. Indicates need for beta-blocker

D. Indicates hypokalemia

Answer: B

Explanation: PAT with block = increased atrial automaticity + AV conduction block = classic digoxin toxicity. Also bidirectional VT. 'Every arrhythmia except rapidly conducted AF'.

  1. Patient on digoxin + amiodarone. Develops toxicity. Why?

A. Amiodarone ↓ digoxin clearance (P-gp inhibition) → ↑ levels

B. Synergistic toxicity

C. Amiodarone displaces digoxin

D. No interaction

Answer: A

Explanation: Amiodarone (also verapamil, quinidine, dronedarone) inhibits P-glycoprotein → ↓ digoxin renal/biliary clearance → ↑ digoxin levels. Reduce digoxin dose 50% if adding amiodarone.

  1. Digoxin Fab given. 1 hour later, K⁺ 2.8. Why?

A. Fab causes hypokalemia

B. Na/K-ATPase reactivated → K⁺ shifts INTO cells rapidly. Monitor and replace.

C. Insulin effect

D. Diuretic effect

Answer: B

Explanation: Fab reverses Na/K-ATPase blockade → K⁺ rapidly shifts into cells → hypokalemia. Monitor K⁺ q1-2h post-Fab. Replace to keep > 4.0.

  1. Digoxin level drawn at 2h post-dose. Level 3 ng/mL. Interpretation?

A. Therapeutic

B. INVALID - must draw at 6h post-dose (distribution complete). Level falsely high at 2h.

C. Toxic

D. Subtherapeutic

Answer: B

Explanation: Digoxin level: draw at 6h post-dose (distribution phase complete). At 2h = falsely high (distribution phase). Also: acute vs chronic thresholds differ.

  1. Digoxin toxicity - calcium for hyperkalemia?

A. Give calcium for membrane stabilization

B. AVOID calcium ('stone heart' - Ca²⁺ overload in digoxin-toxic myocytes)

C. Only if severe ECG changes

D. Calcium gluconate preferred

Answer: B

Explanation: Digoxin + calcium: theoretical risk of 'stone heart' (irreversible contracture) from Ca²⁺ overload in digoxin-toxic myocytes. AVOID calcium. Treat hyperK with Fab.

  1. Digoxin toxicity - temporary pacing?

A. First line for bradycardia

B. Only if hemodynamically unstable awaiting Fab (atropine often ineffective)

C. Contraindicated

D. Only for AV block

Answer: B

Explanation: Atropine often ineffective (not vagal). Temporary pacing if hemodynamically unstable (syncope, hypotension) awaiting Fab. Fab is definitive.

  1. Digoxin Fab - renal failure patient?

A. Contraindicated

B. Can give - Fab-digoxin complex renally cleared but can give in renal failure (monitor for rebound)

C. Reduce dose

D. Only dialysis

Answer: B

Explanation: Fab-digoxin complex renally cleared. In renal failure: can still give Fab (lifesaving). Monitor for rebound toxicity (complex not cleared → dissociates). May need repeat Fab.

24. Flashcards

  • Q: Digoxin mechanism?

A: Inhibits Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase → ↑ intracellular Na⁺ → ↑ Ca²⁺ via Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger (inotropy) + ↑ automaticity + ↓ AV conduction.

  • Q: Digoxin toxicity arrhythmias?

A: 'Every arrhythmia except rapidly conducted atrial arrhythmias (AF with rapid VR)'. Common: PVCs, bigeminy, bidirectional VT, AV block, PAT with block.

  • Q: Bidirectional VT?

A: Pathognomonic for digoxin toxicity. Alternating QRS axis.

  • Q: PAT with block?

A: Atrial tachycardia with AV block = classic digoxin (↑ automaticity + ↓ AV conduction).

  • Q: Fab dose?

A: (Level ng/mL × weight kg) / 100 = vials. Empirical 10-20 vials if level unknown/massive ingestion.

  • Q: Fab indications?

A: Life-threatening arrhythmia, hemodynamic instability, K⁺ > 5.5, level >10 (acute) or >6 (chronic), ingestion >10mg adult/>4mg child.

  • Q: Digoxin hyperkalemia?

A: Na/K-ATPase blocked → K⁺ can't enter cells. Give Fab FIRST. Insulin/dextrose dangerous post-Fab (hypokalemia).

  • Q: Hypokalemia + digoxin?

A: K⁺ competes for Na/K-ATPase. Hypokalemia → ↑ digoxin binding → ↑ toxicity. Correct K⁺ > 4.0.

  • Q: Digoxin + amiodarone?

A: Amiodarone inhibits P-gp → ↓ digoxin clearance → ↑ levels. Reduce digoxin 50%.

  • Q: Atropine for digoxin bradycardia?

A: Often INEFFECTIVE (not vagal). Fab definitive. Temporary pacing if unstable awaiting Fab.

  • Q: Calcium in digoxin hyperK?

A: AVOID - 'stone heart' risk (Ca²⁺ overload in toxic myocytes). Fab for hyperK.

  • Q: Digoxin level timing?

A: Draw at 6h post-dose (distribution complete). Acute >10 toxic, chronic >6 toxic.

  • Q: Post-Fab hypokalemia?

A: Fab reverses Na/K blockade → K⁺ shifts INTO cells → hypokalemia. Monitor q1-2h, replace to >4.0.

  • Q: Fab in renal failure?

A: Can give (lifesaving). Fab-digoxin complex renally cleared → monitor for rebound (dissociation). May need repeat Fab.

  • Q: Digoxin precipitants?

A: Hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, renal failure, elderly, amiodarone/verapamil/quinidine/dronedarone (P-gp inhibitors).

25. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A - Digoxin inhibits Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase → ↑ intracellular Na⁺ → ↑ Ca²⁺ via Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger (inotropy) but also ↑ automaticity, ↓ AV conduction, arrhythmias. Toxicity = same mechanism exaggerated.
  2. B - Digoxin toxicity = 'every arrhythmia except rapidly conducted atrial arrhythmias'. Common: PVCs, bigeminy, VT/VF, AV block (any degree), atrial tachycardia with block, bidirectional VT (pathognomonic).
  3. B - Fab dose = (serum digoxin ng/mL × weight kg) / 100 = number of vials. If level unknown/acute massive: empirical 10-20 vials. Each vial binds ~0.5mg digoxin.
  4. B - Digoxin hyperkalemia = poor prognosis (massive Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase blockade → K⁺ can't enter cells). Give Fab FIRST. Standard hyperK measures can be dangerous (insulin → hypokalemia post-Fab).
  5. B - Bidirectional VT (alternating QRS axis) = pathognomonic for digoxin toxicity. Also: atrial tachycardia with block (PAT with block).
  6. B - Precipitants: hypokalemia (↑ digoxin binding), hypomagnesemia, renal failure (↓ clearance), drug interactions (amiodarone, verapamil, quinidine, dronedarone ↑ levels), elderly (↓ Vd, ↓ renal function).
  7. B - Fab indications: life-threatening arrhythmia, hemodynamic instability, K⁺ > 5.5, level > 10 ng/mL (acute) or > 6 ng/mL (chronic), massive ingestion (>10mg adult, >4mg child).
  8. B - Digoxin bradycardia/AV block = ↓ conduction (not vagal). Atropine often INEFFECTIVE. Fab is definitive. Temporary pacing if hemodynamically unstable awaiting Fab.
  9. A - K⁺ competes with digoxin for Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase binding. Hypokalemia → ↑ digoxin binding → ↑ toxicity. Correct K⁺ > 4.0 (but avoid overcorrection post-Fab).
  10. B - Acute: level > 10 ng/mL toxic. Chronic: > 6 ng/mL toxic (tissue levels higher than serum). Levels at 6h post-dose (distribution complete). Clinical context > level.

SBAs

  1. B - Life-threatening arrhythmia (bidirectional VT) + hyperK = Fab INDICATED. HyperK = Fab FIRST (reverses Na/K-ATPase block). Level 4.5 ng/mL in chronic toxicity = toxic (threshold >6 chronic, but K⁺ 5.8 = Fab indication). Empirical 10-20 vials.
  2. B - Child > 4mg ingestion = massive = Fab indicated empirically. Also: charcoal if <1h. Monitor for arrhythmias, hyperK. Level at 6h post-ingestion.
  3. B - Digoxin hyperK: Na/K-ATPase blocked → K⁺ can't enter cells. Fab reverses blockade → K⁺ shifts INTO cells rapidly. Insulin/dextrose can cause DANGEROUS hypokalemia post-Fab. Give Fab FIRST.
  4. B - PAT with block = increased atrial automaticity + AV conduction block = classic digoxin toxicity. Also bidirectional VT. 'Every arrhythmia except rapidly conducted AF'.
  5. A - Amiodarone (also verapamil, quinidine, dronedarone) inhibits P-glycoprotein → ↓ digoxin renal/biliary clearance → ↑ digoxin levels. Reduce digoxin dose 50% if adding amiodarone.
  6. B - Fab reverses Na/K-ATPase blockade → K⁺ rapidly shifts into cells → hypokalemia. Monitor K⁺ q1-2h post-Fab. Replace to keep > 4.0.
  7. B - Digoxin level: draw at 6h post-dose (distribution phase complete). At 2h = falsely high (distribution phase). Also: acute vs chronic thresholds differ.
  8. B - Digoxin + calcium: theoretical risk of 'stone heart' (irreversible contracture) from Ca²⁺ overload in digoxin-toxic myocytes. AVOID calcium. Treat hyperK with Fab.
  9. B - Atropine often ineffective (not vagal). Temporary pacing if hemodynamically unstable (syncope, hypotension) awaiting Fab. Fab is definitive.
  10. B - Fab-digoxin complex renally cleared. In renal failure: can still give Fab (lifesaving). Monitor for rebound toxicity (complex not cleared → dissociates). May need repeat Fab.

topics.title: Enhanced Elimination Dialysis Hemoperfusion
topics.html:
Related: [[General Principles of Poisoning Management]], [[Antidotes Overview]], [[Gastrointestinal Decontamination]], [[Methanol Poisoning]], [[Ethylene Glycol Poisoning]], [[Salicylate (Aspirin) Poisoning]], [[Lithium Poisoning]], [[Theophylline Poisoning]], [[Metformin Poisoning]], [[Valproate Poisoning]]

[!tip]
Dialysis = gold standard for dialyzable toxins. MDAC for specific enterohepatic recirculation toxins. Urinary alkalinization for weak acids (salicylate, phenobarbital). Key FCPS/MRCP: Dialysis criteria per toxin; drug properties for dialyzability (low protein binding, low Vd, low MW, water soluble); MDAC indications; alkalinization target urine pH 7.5-8.0; ILE for lipophilic cardiotoxic drugs.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Identify indications for hemodialysis/hemoperfusion in poisoning
  • Apply criteria for dialyzability of toxins
  • Prescribe urinary alkalinization protocol
  • Apply MDAC indications
  • Understand ILE (Intralipid) rescue indications

2. Methods of Enhanced Elimination

MethodPrincipleBest For
Intermittent Hemodialysis (IHD)Diffusion across semipermeable membraneSmall, water-soluble, low protein binding, low Vd toxins
Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT)Convection + diffusionHemodynamically unstable; larger molecules
Hemoperfusion (HP)/HemoadsorptionAdsorption onto charcoal/resinProtein-bound, larger molecules (historical: paraquat, phenobarbital)
Plasma Exchange (PLEX/TPE)Removal of plasma + replacementProtein-bound, large Vd (mushroom, TTP-like)
Multiple-Dose Activated Charcoal (MDAC)Gut dialysis + enterohepatic interruptionEnterohepatic recirculation toxins
Urinary AlkalinizationIon trapping in urine (weak acids)Salicylate, phenobarbital, chlorpropamide, methotrexate
Intralipid Emulsion (ILE)Lipid sink + metabolic supportLipophilic cardiotoxic drugs (BB, CCB, TCA, LA)

3. Dialyzability Criteria (The "Dialysis Quartet")

A toxin is well dialyzed if it has ALL FOUR:

  1. Low molecular weight (< 500 Da)
  2. Low protein binding (< 80%, ideally < 50%)
  3. Low volume of distribution (< 1 L/kg, ideally < 0.5 L/kg)
  4. Water soluble (not highly lipid soluble)
Mnemonic: "Lo-Pro-Lo-Vol" (Low MW, Low Protein binding, Low Vd, Water soluble = High dialyzability)

4. Hemodialysis Indications by Toxin

Methanol / Ethylene Glycol — DIALYZE EARLY

  • pH < 7.30 (persistent despite bicarbonate)
  • Renal failure (AKI, oliguria/anuria)
  • Visual symptoms (methanol) — any degree
  • Level > 50 mmol/L (methanol 160 mg/dL; EG 310 mg/dL) — some guidelines > 20 mmol/L with acidosis
  • Refractory electrolyte disturbance
  • Continue until: level < 20 mg/dL (6.2 mmol/L) AND pH normal AND asymptomatic

Salicylate — DIALYZE FOR SEVERE

  • pH < 7.20 (arterial) despite alkalinization
  • Renal failure
  • CNS toxicity (confusion, seizures, coma) — poorly correlates with level
  • Serum level:
  • Acute: > 700 mg/L (70 mg/dL / 5.1 mmol/L)
  • Chronic: > 400 mg/L (40 mg/dL / 2.9 mmol/L)
  • Non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema (refractory)
  • Severe hyperthermia (> 40°C)

Lithium — DIALYZE FOR TOXICITY

  • Acute ingestion: level > 4.0 mmol/L
  • Chronic toxicity: level > 2.5 mmol/L + neuro symptoms / renal impairment / elderly
  • Renal failure (any level)
  • Severe neurotoxicity (seizures, coma)
  • NOTE: Lithium redistributes from tissues → repeat dialysis often needed (rebound)

Theophylline — DIALYZE FOR SEVERE

  • Severe toxicity: seizures, refractory arrhythmias, hypotension
  • Level > 100 mg/L (550 µmol/L) acute
  • Chronic: level > 60 mg/L + toxicity
  • Highly dialyzable (low protein binding, low Vd)

Metformin — DIALYZE FOR LACTIC ACIDOSIS

  • pH < 7.15 (severe lactic acidosis)
  • Lactate > 20 mmol/L
  • Refractory shock
  • Renal failure (metformin accumulates)
  • Highly dialyzable (low protein binding, low Vd)

Valproate — DIALYZE FOR SEVERE

  • Level > 1300 mg/L (9000 µmol/L) or > 900 mg/L with toxicity
  • Coma (GCS < 8)
  • Refractory hypotension/shock
  • Hyperammonemic encephalopathy
  • CRRT preferred (high protein binding but low Vd; CVVH better than IHD)

Ethanol/Isopropyl Alcohol — RARELY DIALYZE

  • Only if severe metabolic acidosis unresponsive, renal failure, or hemodynamic collapse
  • Supportive care usually sufficient

Carbamazepine — MDAC Preferred, Dialysis Refractory

  • MDAC first-line (interrupts enterohepatic recirculation)
  • Dialysis/HP if refractory, coma, refractory seizures, level > 60 mg/L

Phenobarbital — MDAC + Alkalinization + Dialysis

  • MDAC (1g/kg q4-6h)
  • Urinary alkalinization (pH 7.5-8.0)
  • Dialysis/HP if refractory, level > 100 mg/L (acute)

Digoxin — FAB FIRST, DIALYSIS FOR FAB-DIGOXIN COMPLEX

  • Digoxin-specific Fab = primary (see [[Digoxin Poisoning]])
  • Dialysis only if renal failure + Fab unavailable OR Fab-digoxin complex accumulation (anuric)

Other Dialyzable Toxins

  • Methotrexate (high-dose) — dialysis + leucovorin
  • Procainamide — dialysis (NAC analogue)
  • Chloral hydrate — dialysis
  • Sotalol — dialysis (if QT/Torsades refractory)

5. Hemodialysis Practical Considerations

Vascular Access

  • Large-bore catheter (12-14 Fr) — femoral, IJ, or subclavian
  • Adequate flow (> 300 mL/min blood, > 500 mL/min dialysate)

Dialysis Prescription for Poisoning

  • High-efficiency: high-flux dialyzer, max blood/dialysate flow
  • Duration: 4-6 hours per session (longer than renal)
  • Frequency: daily until criteria met
  • Anticoagulation: citrate preferred (regional) or heparin
  • Rebound: expect 20-30% level rebound post-dialysis (tissue redistribution) — repeat if needed

CRRT vs IHD

FeatureIHDCRRT (CVVHDF)
ClearanceHigher peakSustained, lower peak
HemodynamicsMay cause instabilityBetter tolerated
ReboundSignificantLess
Best forStable, small moleculesUnstable, larger molecules (valproate)
AvailabilityOften limitedICU-based

6. Urinary Alkalinization

Principle

  • Weak acids (pKa 3-7) → more ionized in alkaline urine → ion trapping → ↑ renal excretion 10-20x
  • Target urine pH: 7.5-8.0 (check q1-2h with dipstick)

Indications

  1. Salicylate poisoning — primary enhanced elimination
  2. Phenobarbital (pKa 7.2) — adjunct to MDAC/dialysis
  3. Chlorpropamide (sulfonylurea)
  4. Methotrexate (high-dose) — adjunct to leucovorin
  5. Herbicides (2,4-D, mecoprop)

Protocol

  • NaHCO₃ 150 mEq (3 amps 50 mEq) in 1 L D5W
  • Infusion rate: 150-250 mL/hr (adjust to maintain urine pH 7.5-8.0)
  • Add KCl 20-40 mEq/L — hypokalemia impairs alkalinization (K⁺ shifts intracellular)
  • Furosemide 0.5-1 mg/kg if oliguric (maintain UOP > 2 mL/kg/hr)
  • Monitor: urine pH q1-2h, serum pH/pCO₂/K⁺/Na⁺/glucose, fluid balance

Contraindications

  • Severe pulmonary edema / heart failure
  • Renal failure (anuric)
  • Severe metabolic alkalemia (pH > 7.50)

Urinary Acidification — AVOID

  • Not recommended — risks (worsens acidosis, rhabdo, renal failure) outweigh benefits
  • Was used for weak bases (amphetamines, phencyclidine) — no longer standard

7. Multiple-Dose Activated Charcoal (MDAC)

Mechanism

  1. Gut dialysis: charcoal in lumen adsorbs drug diffusing from blood → "dialysis" across gut wall
  2. Enterohepatic interruption: adsorbs drug excreted in bile → prevents reabsorption

Indications (Evidence-Based)

  • Carbamazepine
  • Phenobarbital
  • Theophylline
  • Quinine
  • Dapsone
  • Digitoxin (digoxin: Fab preferred)
  • Amanita phalloides (mushroom) — adjunct

Dosing

  • Initial: 1 g/kg (max 50g) with sorbitol (or without)
  • Maintenance: 1 g/kg (max 25-50g) q4-6h × 2-6 doses without sorbitol
  • Via: NG tube (if not protecting airway, intubate first)
  • Stop: when clinical improvement + falling levels

Contraindications

  • Unprotected airway (aspiration risk)
  • GI obstruction/perforation
  • Ileus
  • Charcoal bezoar risk

8. Intralipid Emulsion (ILE) — Rescue Therapy

Mechanism

  • "Lipid sink": sequesters lipophilic drug in plasma lipid phase
  • Metabolic: provides fatty acid substrate for myocardial energy

Indications

  • Refractory cardiovascular collapse from lipophilic cardiotoxic drugs despite:
  • Vasopressors + antidotes (glucagon, HIET, calcium, Fab, NaHCO₃)
  • Agents: BB (propranolol), CCB (verapamil, amlodipine), TCA, local anesthetics (bupivacaine), others

Dose (20% Lipid Emulsion)

  • Bolus: 1.5 mL/kg (100 mL for 70 kg) over 1 minute
  • Infusion: 0.25 mL/kg/min (1000 mL/hr for 70 kg) for 30-60 minutes
  • Repeat bolus: x2 if persistent arrest (max 10 mL/kg first 30 min)
  • Total max: ~12 mL/kg over 60 min

Monitoring

  • Triglycerides (target < 1000 mg/dL / 11 mmol/L)
  • Fat overload syndrome (rare)
  • Pancreatitis (rare)
  • Interference with lab assays (lipemia)

9. Plasmapheresis / Plasma Exchange (PLEX)

Indications

  • Amanita phalloides (mushroom) — removes amatoxins (protein-bound)
  • TTP/HUS-like syndromes from toxins
  • Highly protein-bound, large Vd toxins where dialysis ineffective

10. Decision Algorithm for Enhanced Elimination

```mermaid

flowchart TD

A[Poisoned Patient] --> B{Life-Threatening Toxicity\nDespite Antidotes/Supportive?}

B -->|No| C[Observe / MDAC if Indicated\nAlkalinization if Indicated]

B -->|Yes| D{Toxin Dialyzable?\n<500 Da, <80% Protein Bind,\n<1 L/kg Vd, Water Soluble}

D -->|Yes| E[Hemodialysis / CRRT\nPer Toxin-Specific Criteria]

D -->|No| F{Lipophilic Cardiotoxic?\nBB, CCB, TCA, Local Anesthetic}

F -->|Yes| G[Intralipid Emulsion Rescue\n1.5mL/kg Bolus + 0.25mL/kg/min]

F -->|No| H{Enterohepatic Recirculation?\nCarbamazepine, Phenobarbital,\nTheophylline, Quinine, Dapsone}

H -->|Yes| I[MDAC 1g/kg q4-6h]

H -->|No| J{Weak Acid?\nSalicylate, Phenobarbital}

J -->|Yes| K[Urinary Alkalinization\nUrine pH 7.5-8.0]

J -->|No| L[Supportive Care\nConsider PLEX if Protein-Bound\nToxicology Consult]

```

11. Suggested Visuals / Image Notes

  • Dialyzability criteria card
  • Dialysis indications table by toxin
  • MDAC/alkalinization/ILE dosing cards
  • Decision algorithm poster

12. Suggested Video References

  • Dialysis for poisoning (Toxbase, Nephrology)
  • ILE rescue demonstration
  • MDAC/alkalinization protocols

13. One-Page Revision Summary

  • Dialyzable: <500 Da, <80% protein bind, <1 L/kg Vd, water soluble (Lo-Pro-Lo-Vol)
  • HD for MeOH/EG: pH<7.3, renal, visual (MeOH), level>50
  • HD for Salicylate: pH<7.2, renal, CNS, level>700/400
  • HD for Lithium: >4 acute, >2.5 chronic+neuro/renal, renal failure
  • HD for Theophylline: seizures/arrhythmia, level>100
  • HD for Metformin: pH<7.15, lactate>20
  • HD for Valproate: coma, level>1300/900, shock (CRRT preferred)
  • MDAC: carbamazepine, phenobarbital, theophylline, quinine, dapsone
  • Alkalinization: salicylate, phenobarbital; target urine pH 7.5-8.0, K⁺ replacement
  • ILE rescue: 1.5mL/kg bolus + 0.25mL/kg/min; BB/CCB/TCA/LA refractory
  • Rebound: expect 20-30% post-HD — repeat if needed

24-Hour Recall Prompts

  • List 4 dialyzability criteria
  • State dialysis criteria for methanol, salicylate, lithium
  • Explain urinary alkalinization target and monitoring
  • State ILE dose and indications

Must Know

  • Dialyzability criteria (Lo-Pro-Lo-Vol)
  • Dialysis criteria per toxin (MeOH/EG, Salicylate, Lithium, Theophylline, Metformin, Valproate)
  • MDAC indications (5 main)
  • Urinary alkalinization: target pH 7.5-8.0, K⁺ replacement
  • ILE rescue: 1.5mL/kg + 0.25mL/kg/min for lipophilic cardiotoxic refractory
  • Rebound phenomenon post-dialysis

Should Know

  • CRRT vs IHD indications
  • Plasmapheresis for Amanita
  • MDAC dosing (no sorbitol after first)
  • Alkalinization contraindications
  • Fab-digoxin complex dialysis

Nice to Know

  • Hemoperfusion historical use
  • Specific dialyzer characteristics
  • Plasmapheresis details
  • Toxin-specific clearance values

16. Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  • Dialyzability criteria
  • Dialysis indications table by toxin
  • MDAC/alkalinization/ILE protocols
  • Practical HD considerations
  • Decision algorithm

Short Note Skeleton

  • Dialyzability box
  • Dialysis criteria table
  • MDAC/alkaline/ILE dosing cards
  • Decision algorithm

Viva One-Liners

  • "Dialyzable: <500 Da, <80% protein, <1 L/kg Vd, water soluble (Lo-Pro-Lo-Vol)"
  • "HD MeOH/EG: pH<7.3, renal, visual, level>50"
  • "HD Salicylate: pH<7.2, renal, CNS, level>700/400"
  • "HD Lithium: >4 acute, >2.5 chronic+neuro/renal, renal failure"
  • "HD Theophylline: seizures/arrhythmia, level>100"
  • "HD Metformin: pH<7.15, lactate>20"
  • "HD Valproate: coma, level>1300, shock (CRRT preferred)"
  • "MDAC: Carbamazepine, Phenobarbital, Theophylline, Quinine, Dapsone"
  • "Alkalinization: Salicylate, Phenobarbital; urine pH 7.5-8.0, K⁺ replacement"
  • "ILE: 1.5mL/kg + 0.25mL/kg/min; BB/CCB/TCA/LA refractory"
  • "Rebound: 20-30% post-HD — repeat if needed"

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • Salicylate pH 7.15 despite alkalinization → HD indicated
  • Lithium 3.0 mmol/L chronic with confusion → HD indicated
  • Valproate coma level 1500 → CRRT preferred
  • Propranolol refractory shock despite glucagon/HIET/NE → ILE
  • Carbamazepine level 60 with coma → MDAC + consider HD

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

  • Dialyzable: Lo-Pro-Lo-Vol
  • HD MeOH/EG: pH<7.3, Renal, Visual, >50
  • HD Sal: pH<7.2, Renal, CNS, >700/400
  • HD Li: >4 acute, >2.5 chronic, Renal
  • HD Theo: Sz/Arrhythmia, >100
  • HD Met: pH<7.15, Lac>20
  • HD Val: Coma, >1300 (CRRT)
  • MDAC: Carba, Phenob, Theo, Quinine, Dapsone
  • Alkalinize: Sal, Phenob; pH 7.5-8.0, K+
  • ILE: 1.5 + 0.25; BB/CCB/TCA/LA
  • Rebound: 20-30%

17. Summary

Enhanced elimination reserved for life-threatening toxicity unresponsive to antidotes/supportive care. Hemodialysis for dialyzable toxins (Lo-Pro-Lo-Vol: <500 Da, <80% protein binding, <1 L/kg Vd, water soluble). Specific criteria per toxin: MeOH/EG (pH<7.3, renal, visual, level>50), salicylate (pH<7.2, renal, CNS, level>700/400), lithium (>4 acute, >2.5 chronic+neuro), theophylline, metformin, valproate (CRRT preferred). MDAC for enterohepatic recirculation toxins (carbamazepine, phenobarbital, theophylline, quinine, dapsone). Urinary alkalinization (pH 7.5-8.0) for weak acids (salicylate, phenobarbital). ILE rescue (1.5mL/kg + 0.25mL/kg/min) for refractory lipophilic cardiotoxic collapse. Expect 20-30% rebound post-HD.
